coredata.dc:title: Electrode modified with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots supported in chitosan for triclocarban monitoring
coredata.dc:creator: Santana, Edson R.
Martins, Eduardo C.
Spinelli, Almir
coredata.dc:description: Triclocarban is an antimicrobial agent frequently used in personal care products with potential activity for endocrine disrupting, and hence its monitoring is particularly important. A sensitive analytical tool was developed for electroanalysis of triclocarban by modifying a glassy carbon substrate with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots immobilized in chitosan. Electron microscopy results showed that the synthesized quantum dots had a diameter of 4.5 nm. The doping with nitrogen in the graphene matrix was confirmed by spectroscopic techniques. Electrochemical techniques showed excellent triclocarban oxidation current responses provided by the modified electrode compared to the bare electrode. The calibration plot for triclocarban was constructed in 0.1 mol L−1 phosphate buffer (pH 7.0):ethanol (95:5 v/v) in the range of 0.05 to 8.0 μmol L−1 with limits of detection and quantification of 17.0 and 57.0 nmol L−1, respectively. The modified electrode was effectively implemented in the quantification of triclocarban in personal care products with recoveries data in the range of 90 to 109%. Furthermore, the modified electrode was applied in the simultaneous determination of triclosan (+0.60 V) and triclocarban (+0.95 V) with limits of detection of 14.0 and 21.0 nmol L−1, respectively. The application was carried out in surface water samples with excellent accuracy indicated by recovery data in the range of 93 to 110%.

Cyclic voltammograms for 10.0 μmol L−1 TCC obtained with the N-GQD-CS/GCE in 0.1 mol L−1B-R buffer:ethanol (95:5 v/v) at different pH values (A) pH 2.0 to 7.0 and (B) pH 8.0 to 12.0; ν = 50.0 mV s−1. (C) (a) Current and (b) potential vs. pH (n = 3).

(A) Differential pulse voltammograms for different concentrations of TCC obtained with the N-GQD-CS/GCE in 0.1 mol L−1 phosphate buffer (pH 7.0):ethanol (95:5 v/v). Calibration plot for TCC (n = 3). DPV conditions: scan rate = 30.0 mV s−1; amplitude = 40.0 mV; and pulse time = 5.0 ms.

(A) Differential pulse voltammograms for different concentrations of TCC and TCS obtained with the N-GQD-CS/GCE in 0.1 mol L−1 phosphate buffer (pH 7.0):ethanol (95:5 v/v). Calibration plots for TCS (curve a) and TCC (curve b) (n = 3). DPV conditions: scan rate = 30.0 mV s−1; amplitude = 40.0 mV; and time pulse = 5.0 ms.

head.ce:title: Electrode modified with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots supported in chitosan for triclocarban monitoring

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Triclocarban is an antimicrobial agent frequently used in personal care products with potential activity for endocrine disrupting, and hence its monitoring is particularly important. A sensitive analytical tool was developed for electroanalysis of triclocarban by modifying a glassy carbon substrate with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots immobilized in chitosan. Electron microscopy results showed that the synthesized quantum dots had a diameter of 4.5 nm. The doping with nitrogen in the graphene matrix was confirmed by spectroscopic techniques. Electrochemical techniques showed excellent triclocarban oxidation current responses provided by the modified electrode compared to the bare electrode. The calibration plot for triclocarban was constructed in 0.1 mol L−1 phosphate buffer (pH 7.0):ethanol (95:5 v/v) in the range of 0.05 to 8.0 μmol L−1 with limits of detection and quantification of 17.0 and 57.0 nmol L−1, respectively. The modified electrode was effectively implemented in the quantification of triclocarban in personal care products with recoveries data in the range of 90 to 109%. Furthermore, the modified electrode was applied in the simultaneous determination of triclosan (+0.60 V) and triclocarban (+0.95 V) with limits of detection of 14.0 and 21.0 nmol L−1, respectively. The application was carried out in surface water samples with excellent accuracy indicated by recovery data in the range of 93 to 110%.

ce:section.ce:para: Triclocarban (3,4,4′-trichlorocarbanilide, TCC) is a broad-spectrum antimicrobial agent frequently used in personal care products [1]. TCC is an aromatic compound with a high chlorine content in its structure, which can lead to biodegradation resistance and thus environmental persistence [2]. Additionally, TCC is a lipophilic compound, which explains its potential for bioaccumulation in the food chain [2]. Thus, human exposure to TCC occurs not only directly through skin contact with products, such as soap and toothpaste, but also through the intake of contaminated food and drinking water [3]. Studies have shown that TCC poses several health risks, including endocrine disruption and reproductive and developmental toxicity [4]. Recent studies have warned that human exposure to TCC may increase the risk of type 2 diabetes mellitus in the women and promote of liver tumor progression [5,6]. Given that TCC can be found in several matrices and presents a risk to the environment and human health, the monitoring of this compound is an important area of research. Furthermore, its determination in commercial samples would facilitate inspection by competent agencies.

Many studies have been carried out employing chromatographic methods to quantify TCC in different matrices [7–9]. However, there is a shortage of studies using other analytical methods for this purpose. On this topic, only two studies could be found in the literature, aimed at quantifying TCC by ELISA [10] and using an electrochemical method [11]. In the case of the electrochemical method, Lúcio and co-authors [11] reported the determination of TCC in personal care products and drinking water samples using square wave voltammetry (SWV). The measurements were carried out using a bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE). The limit of detection for TCC obtained by the authors by SWV was 1.8 × 10−7 mol L−1. Electroanalytical techniques, such as voltammetry, are capable of furnishing low detection limits without requiring a high cost of analysis, in addition to providing easy operation as well as the portability of the equipment [12–16]. The sensitivity of an electroanalytical assay can be improved with the use of modified electrodes [17–22]. This analytical tool can be obtained by immobilizing a variety of materials (inorganic and organic) on the substrate surface [23,24].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which consist of a few graphene sheets smaller than 100 nm, have been used as electrode modifying materials [25]. Some interesting properties of GQDs, such as high surface area, low cytotoxicity, excellent solubility in various solvents, appropriate conductivity and synthesis from abundant raw materials of low cost, have led to GQDs being explored in several areas of research [25–28]. Doping of GQDs with nitrogen can modify their electronic and chemical features, optimizing the surface structure and enhancing the electrochemical performance [29,30]. Nitrogen has a similar atomic radius and greater electronegativity compared with carbon, properties that make it easier to incorporate nitrogen into the carbon network via substitution doping [30]. Electrodes based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have been reported for the electrochemical determination of nitroaromatic compounds, including 2,4,6-trinitrophenol [31] and 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene [32], biomolecules, such as dopamine [33] and cholesterol [34] and inorganic ions like Hg2+ [35].

Herein, we describe the application of an N-GQD-based electrode for the quantification of TCC in personal care products. As N-GQDs are water-soluble, for the modification of the GCE, the use of chitosan (CS) was essential for the formation of a stable film containing the N-GQDs. In addition, the applicability of N-GQD-CS/GCE was also tested for the simultaneous determination of TCC and triclosan (TCS). Like TCC, TCS is an antimicrobial (antibacterial and/or antifungal) agent widely used in personal care products [36]. As both compounds have the same function in personal care products, it is not common to find them simultaneously in a specific product [36]. However, both compounds are persistent and can be found in various environmental compartments, particularly those associated with domestic wastewater and industrial sewage [37]. In that regard, Halden and Paull [38] noted concern regarding the frequency at which TCC and TCS were detected in surface water samples in the United States. Because of incomplete removal, spills, and leakage from sewage during municipal wastewater treatment, the authors determined TCC and TCS at levels of µg L−1 in surface water samples. Because of these types of sewage discharges, TCC and TCS were also detected at levels from ng L−1 to µg L−1 in surface water samples in several countries [39].

Chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich/Merck (Germany) and used without further purification. Citric acid anhydrous (≥99.5%) and tris(hydroxymethyl)amino-methane (≥99.8%) were used for the synthesis of N-GQDs. The chitosan employed had a low molecular weight (75–85% deacetylated). The solutions were prepared with water purified in a Milli-Q system (Millipore, USA) with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm. Stock solutions of 1.0 mmol L−1 triclocarban (99%) and triclosan (CRM) were prepared in ethanol (99.5%). Three buffers were tested as the supporting electrolyte: Britton-Robinson (B-R) (H3BO3, >99.5%/CH3COOH, >99%/H3PO4, 85%), phosphate buffer (NaH2PO4, ≥98.0%/Na2HPO4, ≥99.0%), and McIlvaine (citric acid/Na2HPO4). The pH adjustments were performed with 1.0 mol L−1 HCl (37%) or NaOH (>97%). The 0.1 mol L−1 acetic acid was used for the preparation of a 0.25% (w/v) chitosan solution. Due to the low solubility of TCC in water, a buffer:ethanol (95:5 v/v) system was used as the supporting electrolyte. All the solutions were stored under refrigeration at 5 °C.

The N-GQDs were obtained as described by Kashani et al. [40]. This one-step procedure involves pyrolysis with citric acid (CA) as a carbon source and tris(hydroxymethyl)amino-methane (tris-HMA) as a nitrogen dopant. In a 5.0 mL beaker, 1.0 g of CA and 0.25 g of tris-HMA were mixed and heated to 160 °C (kept for 15 min) using a hotplate. Transparent molten CA was observed after approximately 3 min of heating. The color of the molten substance changed to orange after 8 min of heating, designating the formation of N-GQDs. After cooling to room temperature, 5.0 mL of ultrapure water was added to dissolve the orange solid obtained. For neutralization to pH 7.0, 1.0 mol L−1 NaOH was added drop by drop to the dispersion. The dispersion was then left in a dialysis bag overnight, to completely remove salt ions (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da), yielding pale yellow N-GQDs. The N-GQD dispersion was diluted to 25.0 mL for use in the experiments.

N-GQDs were characterized by attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR FT-IR) using a Spectrum-100 spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA) in the range of 550 to 4000 cm−1. The N-GQD dispersion was frozen and subsequently lyophilized for the ATR FT-IR studies. A UV-1800 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and a quartz cell (with a 1.0 cm optical path) were used for the optical characterization of the N-GQD dispersion. The emission and excitation spectra for the N-GQD dispersion were carried out on an Infinite M200 fluorimeter (Tecan, Austria) in a 1.0 cm quartz cuvette. The characterization of the N-GQD dispersion by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was registered by a high-resolution transmission electron microscope, model JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan), operating at 100 kV. For the TEM analysis, an aliquot of 5.0 μL of the N-GQD dispersion was dripped onto a millimetric carbon-coated copper grid (300 mesh), which was kept for 24 h at room temperature for complete drying.

To develop the modified electrode, 200 μL of the N-GQD dispersion was added to 9.8 mL of a 0.25% (m/v) chitosan solution and kept at 60 °C for 30 min under stirring. After, 2.0 μL of the N-GQD-CS dispersion was dripped onto the GCE surface. For this procedure, the GCE was previously polished with aluminum oxide (0.05 μm), washed, and subjected to an ultrasonic bath for 3 min in ultrapure water. The N-GQD-CS/GCE modified electrode was allowed to dry in an oven at 40 °C.

The electrochemical experiments were carried out on a portable potentiostat PalmSens 4 (Palm Instruments BV, The Netherlands). A cell containing three electrodes was used in the experiments: N-GQD-CS/GCE as the working electrode (geometric area = 0.07 cm2), Ag/AgCl (KCl saturated) as the reference electrode and a platinum wire as the counter electrode. The differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was applied with the followed optimized parameters: scan rate 30.0 mV s−1, pulse time 5.0 ms and amplitude 40.0 mV. After each electrochemical measurement, it was necessary to clean the electrode surface and prepare a new film. This procedure was executed due to partial blockage of the electrode surface and electroactive products generated from the oxidation of TCC, which could interfere with subsequent measures [11].

The same potentiostat was used for the characterization of the N-GQD-CS/GCE by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The EIS spectra were recorded at open circuit potential using a 5.0 mmol L−1 equimolar mixture of K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1 mol L−1 KCl. An amplitude value of 5.0 mV and a frequency range of 0.1 to 50,000 Hz were applied.

The data generated with the N-GQD-CS/GCE were compared with the results acquired applying UV–vis spectrometry. The spectra were recorded in the wavelength range of 220 to 320 nm. The calibration plot for TCC was constructed (in ethanol) using the wavelength of maximum absorbance of 265 nm.

The N-GQD-CS/GCE was applied in the quantification of TCC in personal care products. Commercial bar and liquid soap samples produced by two companies (A and B) were purchased in stores in Florianópolis, Brazil. The preparation of the samples was based on a previous study by our group [25]: 0.5 g of the soap samples was subjected to an ultrasonic bath in 10.0 mL of ethanol for 40 min. The suspension obtained was filtered (25.0 μm) and diluted in ethanol in a 25.0 mL volumetric flask for further analysis.

The N-GQD-CS/GCE was also applied in the quantification of TCC and TCS in surface water. The surface water samples were collected from the North Bay (seawater, 27°35′10.7″S 48°33′11.7″W) and Conceição Lagoon (brackish water, 27°36′32.5″S 48°26′57.2″W) in Florianópolis, Brazil. The samples were filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane filter after collection. For the assays, the samples were prepared in a 10.0 mL volumetric flask with a surface water:ethanol (95:5 v/v) system and then saved in Falcon tubes and kept at 5 °C. Each sample was then sonicated for 10 min, until complete homogenization.

The reliability of the data provided by the N-GQD-CS/GCE electrode was assessed. The limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) were calculated based on the standard deviation (SD) of the intercept and the slope of the calibration plot (s), employing the equations LOD = 3 × (SD/s) and LOQ = 10 × (SD/s) [41]. The influence of matrix components was assessed by comparing the slopes of the calibration and the standard addition plots. The slopes of the two plots are expected to be similar if there are no matrix effects during the assays. The intra-day and inter-day repeatability were carried out to assess precision of the modified electrode through the analysis of five replicates containing 1.0 μmol L−1 TCC. The precision was expressed in terms of relative standard deviation (RSD). Accuracy of the method was determined by recovery experiments, in which recovery percentages close to 100% are expected [41]. The recovery assays were carried out by spiking the crude samples of personal care products with 1.0 μmol L−1 of TCC and the crude samples of surface water with 1.0 μmol L−1 of TCS and TCC. The recovery ranges encompass the lowest percentage and the highest percentage obtained in analyzes of five replicates of each sample. In other words, five recovery assays were carried out with each sample, obtaining five recovery percentages. The recovery range reported contains the extreme values obtained for each sample. The concentrations in the samples were determined by the standard addition method. The reliability of the data obtained from the electrochemical method was assessed by applying paired t and F tests to the data obtained with the UV–vis spectrometry.

The ATR FT-IR spectrum of N-GQDs (Fig. S1A) shows stretching vibrations at 3380 cm−1 (−NH) and 3232 cm−1 (−OH). The doping with nitrogen atoms on the graphene quantum dots structure was confirmed by the presence of stretching vibration bands at 1640 cm−1 (C=N) and 1380 cm−1 (C–NH) [40,42]. In addition, the vibration peaks at 1730, 1190 and 1005 cm−1 can be ascribed to C=C stretching, C–O stretching and C–O bending [40]. As verified by the FT-IR spectrum, functional groups like –COOH, –OH, and –NH on the surface of the N-GQDs improve the hydrophilicity and stability of the N-GQDs in an aqueous system [40]. The TEM image (Fig. S1B) shows that the N-GQDs were uniform and well dispersed. The mean diameter of the N-GQDs was estimated to be around 4.5 nm (inset Fig. S1B), which is consistent with other studies in which N-GQDs were synthesized through CA/tris-HMA pyrolysis [40,43].

In the UV–vis absorption spectra for citric acid and tris-HMA (precursors) no absorption bands were present, while in the spectrum for the N-GQDs there was a shoulder peak at 230 nm and a well-defined absorption band at 330 nm (Fig. S2A). The peak at 230 nm was attributed to the π–π* transitions of the C=C bonds [43]. The strong absorbance at 330 nm, which results from n–π* transition of C=O or C−OH bond in sp3 hybrid regions, originates from the trapping of excited state energy of the surface, which can lead to strong fluorescence [44]. The fluorescence of the N-GQDs (Fig. S2B) showed maximum intensity at the emission wavelength of 420 nm when the excitation wavelength was set at 340 nm. Furthermore, the emission-independence of N-GQDs, since the excitation wavelength was varied from 300 nm to 380 nm, was attributed to the fact that both the size and the surface state of the sp2 clusters contained in its structure will be uniform [43].

The N-GQD-CS/GCE showed superior results when compared to bare GCE and CS/GCE using CV and EIS techniques. An electrode with only N-GQDs could not be constructed since there was no formation of a stable film on the substrate. Regarding the data obtained by CV (Fig. S3A), the current responses (anodic and cathodic) of the N-GQD-CS/GCE were around twice as high as the CS/GCE data and approximately five times higher than the bare GCE responses. The better performance of the CS/GCE can be attributed to the affinity of the positively-charged chitosan with the negative charges of the pair [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– contributing to the adsorption of the species on the surface of the electrode [25]. However, when N-GQDs are stabilized in chitosan, there is an increase in current also associated with an improvement in the electrical conductivity and higher surface area provided by the N-GQDs [25].

Another indication of the improvement in the electrode performance through the modification of the surface with N-GQD-CS was based on the resistance to charge transfer (Rct) results obtained in the EIS study. The Rct values fitted from the Nyquist plots (Fig. S3B) were 356, 237 and 98 Ω for GCE, CS/GCE and N-GQD-CS/GCE, respectively. The decrease in the Rct values demonstrated that electron transfer was favored between the [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– probe and the electrode interface. Although chitosan has been considered as a nonconductive polymer, the improvement in electron transfer observed for the CS/GCE can be attributed to the electrostatic interaction between the positively charged chitosan and the negatively charged [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− probe [25]. However, the most effective contribution to the improvement in electron transfer in the system was the presence of N-GQDs. The Rct value provided by the N-GQD-CS/GCE was the lowest of the electrodes tested, indicating that the presence of N-GQDs can significantly improve the conductivity and facilitate the electron transfer between the probe and the electrode interface.

To assess the response of the proposed analytical tool obtained in the monitoring of TCC, the performance of the N-GQD-CS/GCE was compared to that of the bare and partially modified electrode (Fig. 1 A). For the bare GCE (curve a), a peak was observed at +0.97 V with a low current of 0.56 μA, corresponding to the oxidation of TCC. The peak observed is attributed to the oxidation of –NH of one of the amide groups presented in the TCC structure [11]. Using the GCE coated with CS (CS/GCE, curve b), the current increased by a factor of two. The best response was provided by the electrode modified with the N-GQDs supported in CS (curve c) with a four-fold increase in the oxidation current compared with the bare GCE (curve a). An electrode comprised of only N-GQDs could not be evaluated, since there was no formation of a stable film on the substrate. However, on evaluating the results obtained with the CS/GCE and N-GQD-CS/GCE, the gain in response can be attributed to the increased electroactive area of the modified electrode, the conductivity provided by the N-GQDs, and the increase in the rate of charge transfer to the electrode [25]. The peak current decrease while recording successive scans (data not shown) was due to TCC oxidation products that could have caused fouling and inactivation of the available electrode surface area [11]. Thus, after each recorded measurement, it was necessary to clean the electrode surface and prepare a new film of N-GQD-CS.

The electrochemical behavior of the TCC was monitored at different scan rates using the N-GQD-CS/GCE (Fig. 1B). The absence of the reverse peaks in all voltammograms recorded can be attributed to an irreversible process for TCC oxidation. With data obtained from the scan rate study, the plot of the logarithm of the peak current versus the logarithm of the scan rate (Fig. S4A, axis a) provided a slope value of 0.78, indicating that the electrochemical process is adsorption-limited. According the literature, the slope must be 1.0 for adsorption control and 0.5 for processes controlled by diffusion [45]. In addition, the relationship between potential peak (Ep) vs. log ν (Fig. S4A, axis b) furnished a slope value of 0.121. According to the Laviron theory [46], the slope of Ep vs. log ν is equivalent to 2.303RT/(αzF) for a totally irreversible electrode reaction process, where R is the universal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1), T is the temperature (298 K), α is the transfer coefficient, z is the number of electrons transferred and F is the Faraday constant (96,485 C mol−1). Generally, α is assumed to be 0.5 for irreversible electrode processes [45]. Thus, the electron transfer number (z) for the oxidation of TCC was assumed to be 1. This result corroborates the data reported by Lúcio and co-authors [11] and represents the oxidation of one of the amide groups of the TCC molecule. Lastly, the current function (ip ν−1/2) increases with increasing scan rate (Fig. S4B), indicating the occurrence of a coupled chemical reaction after the electron transfer reaction [47]. In this regard, Baumann and co-authors [48] observed the formation of 10 products of TCC oxidation, which may be a consequence of these coupled chemical reactions. The authors used an electrochemical cell in line with a liquid chromatography - electrospray ionization mass spectrometry system for observation of TCC oxidation products. However, the accurate identification of the oxidation mechanism for TCC requires further study.

The influence of the pH of the supporting electrolyte on the electrochemical reaction of 10.0 μmol L−1 TCC was examined in the pH range of 2.0 to 12.0 (Fig. 2 A and 2B). Fig. 2C (axis a) highlights how the anodic peak currents vary as a function of the pH of the supporting electrolyte. It can be observed that the current values increased from pH 2.0 to 7.0 and decreased at alkaline pH. Thus, in order to obtain a better sensitivity for the determination of TCC, pH 7.0 was adopted for the subsequent analysis. In addition, a linear shift of the E versus pH plot (Fig. 2C, axis b) was observed, with a slope of −56.3 mV pH−1, which was close to the theoretical value of −59.2 mV pH−1 for the Nernst equation [45]. These data indicate that the same number of mols of electrons and protons are transferred during the oxidation of TCC on the surface of the modified electrode. Thus, by also considering the previous scan rate study (Fig. 2B), it can be proposed that the reaction occurs through the transfer of one mol of protons and one mol of electrons. Furthermore, the shift of the peak potential over the entire pH range studied is consistent with the fact that TCC has a pKa value of 12.7 and remains protonated under the conditions studied [1].

Subsequently, pH values of 6.0, 7.0 and 8.0 were used to evaluate the influence of different compositions of supporting electrolytes on the electrochemical response to TCC. Due to the low solubility of TCC in water a buffer:ethanol (95:5 v/v) system was adopted. The solutions tested were B-R, phosphate and McIlvaine, all at a concentration of 0.1 mol L−1. The highest current values were obtained in phosphate buffer at pH 7.0 (Fig. S5). Therefore, this buffered solution was used in the subsequent analytical experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration plot obtained by electroanalytical method
ce:section.ce:para: Under the best conditions, the calibration plot for TCC was obtained using the N-GQD-CS/GCE by DPV in the concentration range of 0.05 to 8.0 µmol L−1 (Fig. 3 A). A defined peak close to +0.96(±0.04) V can be observed, which is consistent with irreversible TCC oxidation. The calibration plot for TCC is shown in Fig. 3B.

The linear regression equation is given by: I/µA = 2.96(±0.04)[TCC]/µmol L−1 − 0.01(±1.70 × 10−2) (r = 0.995), where I is the resulting peak current in µA and [TCC] is the concentration of triclocarban in µmol L−1. The LOD and LOQ values were 17.0 and 57.0 nmol L−1, respectively.

For comparative purposes, a calibration plot was also produced using a bare GCE under identical experimental conditions (data not shown). This plot was linear from 0.5 to 14.0 µmol L−1 with the equation: I/µA = 0.94(±1.00 × 10−3)[TCC]/µmol L−1 + 0.60(±0.50). The LOD and LOQ values were 160 and 530 nmol L−1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration plot obtained by UV–vis spectrometry
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the N-GQD-CS/GCE results, they were compared with the values obtained using UV–vis spectrometry. Calibration plots were constructed with standard solutions containing different concentrations of TCC in ethanol (Fig. S6A). The calibration plot (Fig. S6B) was linear in the concentration range of 1.0 to 20.0 μmol L−1 (r = 0.999) with an equation of A = 4.10 × 10−2(±2.58 × 10−4)[TCC]/µmol L−1 + 1.15 × 10−2(±4.50 × 10−3). The LOD and LOQ values were 330 and 1100 nmol L−1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: An overview of reported methods for TCC monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical methods reported for the quantification of TCC are mainly chromatographic, as seen in Table 1 . Although the LOD obtained using the N-GQD-CS/GCE is higher than the limits obtained by chromatographic methods, the development and operation of the proposed electrode are simpler, and the equipment required is more accessible. In addition, voltammetric measurements are faster compared to a chromatographic run, which allows a higher analytical frequency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Repeatability, stability, and selectivity studies of the N-GQD-CS/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The repeatability of the current supplied by the N-GQD-CS/GCE was tested through an intra-day assay, using five standard solutions of 1.0 μmol L−1 TCC prepared on the same day, and an inter-day assay, conducted over five days using a solution of 1.0 μmol L−1 TCC prepared each day. The RSD values for the measurements (n = 5) were 4.1% and 5.3%, respectively. The RSD values obtained for the intra-day and inter-day assays indicated excellent repeatability of the data obtained with the N-GQD-CS/GCE.

The stability of the N-GQD-CS dispersion was investigated over a period of five-weeks. The dispersion was stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator when it was not in use, but it was removed and left to reach ambient temperature before preparing the electrode. The current responses to 1.0 μmol L−1 TCC provided by the N-GQD-CS/GCE prepared with the same dispersion decreased by around 3.2% after five weeks, when compared with the first current response. This result indicates that the N-GQD-CS dispersion remained stable during this period and thus it can be used for the preparation of modified electrodes for at least five weeks.

To evaluate the selectivity provided by the N-GQD-CS/GCE electrode and possible interference from organic molecules and inorganic ions affecting the data obtained, DPV was applied in the presence of potential interferents in 0.1 mol L−1 phosphate buffer (pH 7.0):ethanol (95:5 v/v) containing 1.0 μmol L−1 TCC (+0.96 V). The concentration of organic compounds was 50 times higher than that of TCC. Ascorbic acid, uric acid, dopamine, rutin, catechin and tannic acid were oxidized at potentials less positive than +0.6 V, while coumarin showed no oxidation reaction. The decrease in the oxidation currents for TCC was lower than 8% in the presence of the potential interferents. The voltammograms for this study are shown in the Fig. S7 in the absence and presence of TCC. In addition, no significant interference from common ions, such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl−, NO3 − and SO4 2−, was observed even with 100-fold excess concentrations. These results indicate that the proposed modified electrode has good selectivity toward TCC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TCC in personal care products
ce:section.ce:para: The TCC contents in four samples of personal care products were quantified by DPV using the N-GQD-CS/GCE and the results were compared with data obtained by UV–vis spectrometry (Table 2 ). To minimize the matrix effects, the standard addition method was used in both techniques for the quantification. The TCC contents determined in all assays are below the limit established by Brazilian regulatory agencies. ANVISA regulated the addition of TCC as antimicrobial agent at concentrations of up to 1.5% (m/m) in personal care products that can be rinsed off (e.g. soaps) (RDC 3/2012) [53], and a maximum limit of 0.2% (m/m) if the TCC is used as preservative in commercial products as cosmetic and perfumes (RDC 29/2012) [54]. Recovery experiments by both methods were between 90 and 110%. These results confirm the accuracy of the data provided by both analytical procedures.

In addition, statistical tests were applied to compare the sets of data obtained applying the electrochemical and spectrometric methods (Table S1). The F-test was used to compare the precision of the results obtained with the two methods. The calculated F values were lower than Fcritical, indicating that there is no significant difference between the precision of the data obtained with the two procedures. For the comparison of the means provided by the two methods, the paired t-test was applied. All values for t were lower than the tcritical value, indicating that there were no systematic errors in the data provided by the two methods.

The results for one of the replicates of the bar soap sample obtained applying DPV with the N-GQD-CS/GCE are shown in Fig. S8. The differential pulse voltammograms are shown in Fig. S8A for the blank (curve a), bar soap sample (curve b) and the sample with successive additions of the TCC standard solution (c–f curves). The standard addition plot (Fig. S8B – curve a) is presented along with the previously constructed calibration plot (Fig. S8B – curve b). It can be observed that the slopes of the standard addition and calibration plots are similar, which means that the matrix components did not interfere in the TCC determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous determination of TCC and TCS in surface water
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the N-GQD-CS/GCE for the simultaneous determination of TCC and TCS in surface water was also tested. A calibration plot for this simultaneous analysis was thus constructed using the N-GQD-CS/GCE. The differential pulse voltammograms (Fig. 4A) showed that the oxidation peaks of TCS (+0.60 ± 0.06 V) and TCC (+0.95 ± 0.05 V) were far enough away for simultaneous analysis. The calibration plots (Fig. 4B) were linear in the concentration range of 0.05 to 8.0 μmol L−1, with equations of I/µA = 4.20(±0.06)[TCS]/µmol L−1 + 0.06(±0.02) (r = 0.998) and I/µA = 2.85(±0.03)[TCC]/µmol L−1 + 0.02(±0.02) (r = 0.997). The LOD and LOQ values were calculated, respectively, as 14.0 and 48.0 nmol L−1 for TCS and 21.0 and 70.0 nmol L−1 for TCC. The similarity between the slope values of the calibration plots for the individual (Fig. 3B) and simultaneous (Fig. 4 B – curve b) determination of TCC, indicated that there was no interference of TCS in the simultaneous assays.

In addition, DPV curves were recorded at different concentrations of one compound while the concentration of the other compound remained constant at 0.8 µmol L−1 (Fig. S9). Two defined peaks at +0.60 ± 0.08 V and +0.93 ± 0.09 V, corresponding to the oxidations of TCS and TCC, respectively, were observed. On maintaining the concentration of TCC constant (Fig. S9A), the anodic current of TCS increased linearly with its concentration in the range of 0.05 to 8.0 µmol L−1. The linear regression equation obtained for the calibration plot was I/µA = 4.17(±0.07)[TCS]/µmol L−1 − 0.10(±0.02) (r = 0.997) (Fig. S9B). Similarly, as shown in Fig. S9C, the anodic current value increased linearly with the TCC concentration in the range of 0.05 to 8.0 µmol L−1, with a linear regression equation of I/µA = 2.77(±0.04)[TCC]/µmol L−1 − 0.13(±0.02) (r = 0.996) (Fig. S9D). These results indicated that the sensitivity of the N-GQD-CS/GCE electrode did not change significantly during the simultaneous determination of TCS and TCC when compared with the quantification of the same compounds individually in the presence of a fixed concentration of the other compound.

The simultaneous determination of the two compounds TCC and TCS, in two surface water samples: seawater (sample A) and brackish water (sample B) (Table S2), was evaluated. Neither compound was detected in the crude samples of surface water. The recovery results obtained ranged from 93 to 105% for TCS and 95 to 108% for TCC for sample A. In the case of sample B, the results were 94 to 110% and 97 to 108% for TCS and TCC, respectively. These recovery results indicate that excellent accuracy is provided by the N-GQD-CS/GCE in the simultaneous determination of TCC and TCS in surface water. In addition, the similarity between the slope values of the standard addition and calibration plots in these assays (data not shown), indicates that the matrix components did not interfere in the simultaneous determination of TCC and TCS.

TCS and TCC have been detected in real water samples in concentrations ranging from ng L−1 to µg L−1 around the world [39]. Some studies showed ng L−1 levels of TCS, such as 415 ng L−1 [55], and 35.2 ng L−1 [56] in Brazilian rivers. In the United Kingdom, a TCS level of 463 µg L−1 was reported in an influent of wastewater treatment plant (WWTP) [57]. Regarding TCC levels, studies showed levels of 6.18 µg L−1 in an Indian river [58], and 6.75 µg L−1 in an urban stream in the United States [38]. In addition, lower levels of TCC were also detected, such as a concentration of 127 ng L−1 in an effluent of WWTP in Spain [59], and 46.2 ng L−1 in a Chinese river [60]. In our work, it was clearly demonstrated that TCS and TCC can be detected at ng L−1 levels and quantified at µg L−1 levels using the proposed modified electrode. In this regard, the N-GQD-CS/GCE could provide an alternative analytical tool to aid the monitoring of these antimicrobial agents in water samples.

An electroanalytical method using an N-GQD/CS based electrode was developed and applied in the determination of TCC in personal care products. This is the first modified electrode developed for the electroanalysis of TCC. The presence of N-GQDs facilitated charge transfer in the system providing higher current intensities and lower resistance to charge transfer when compared to bare GCE. The chitosan was fundamental for the formation of a stable film on the glassy carbon substrate. New N-GQD-CS films were required for each electrochemical measurement due to the adsorption of TCC oxidation products. The LOD obtained for TCC was 17.0 nmol L−1, which allowed the determination of TCC in personal care products. The reliability of the data provided by the N-GQD-based electrode was confirmed considering data obtained by UV–vis spectrometry and endorsed by statistical tests. In addition, the N-GQD-CS/GCE furnished LOD values of 14.0 and 21.0 nmol L−1 for simultaneous determination of TCS and TCC, respectively. The applicability of N-GQD-CS/GCE for the simultaneous monitoring of these compounds was tested by analyzing surface waters with excellent accuracy provided by the modified electrode, which was verified by recovery test results.
